In June 1914, a parade organized by a dissident faction of Butte's miners — sympathizers with the IWW who believed the established Western Federation of Miners had become too conservative and too close to the copper company — turned violent. On June 13, the dissidents dynamited the Miners Union Hall, destroying the building that had been the institutional center of Butte's organized labor movement since the 1870s and burning thousands of irreplaceable union records.

The riot and the hall's destruction marked a critical fracture in Butte's labor movement. The Western Federation of Miners had been the dominant union force in Butte since the WFM's founding in 1893, with Butte's Local No. 1 as its flagship chapter. The WFM had won significant concessions from the mining companies on wages and working conditions, but by 1914 its leadership was seen by a radicalized faction as complicit with Anaconda's anti-labor policies.

The Anaconda Company seized on the disorder to withdraw recognition from all unions — a victory the company had sought for years. Without union protection, miners worked under deteriorating safety conditions that contributed directly to the Speculator Mine disaster three years later. The WFM itself eventually merged with the United Mine Workers, while the IWW continued as a separate radical presence in Butte through the World War I years.

The 1914 riots and the subsequent loss of union recognition were the most significant setback for organized labor in Butte's history, setting the stage for the violent confrontations of 1917 and cementing the copper company's dominance over the city's political and economic life for another generation.

headline: The Anaconda Copper Mining Company — A Corporate Giant That Owned a State
summary:
## From Marcus Daly's Hunch to One of History's Greatest Industrial Monopolies

The Anaconda Copper Mining Company began in 1881 when Marcus Daly bought a modest silver mine named the Anaconda on a hill above Butte and discovered it sat atop an enormous copper deposit. With backing from George Hearst and his partners, Daly developed the mine, built a smelter in the company town of Anaconda, and connected it to Butte by private railroad. Within a decade, Anaconda was producing the bulk of American copper output and Daly was one of the wealthiest men in the country.

In 1899, Daly merged with the Standard Oil interests of William Rockefeller and Henry H. Rogers to form the Amalgamated Copper Mining Company, which was reorganized in 1915 as the Anaconda Copper Mining Company in its final form. Under the Rockefeller-era management and then under John D. Ryan and Cornelius Kelley's long leadership, Anaconda expanded far beyond mining: the company purchased all major Montana daily newspapers (retaining them until 1959), owned the Missoula mercantile company, controlled power and utility companies, and became — in the phrase commonly used in Montana — "the Company" that shaped state politics, regulated media coverage, and determined the fortunes of hundreds of thousands of people.

At its peak in the 1920s, Anaconda was the fourth-largest company in the world. It expanded into Chile, Mexico, and Africa, becoming a major force in global copper markets. Its Montana operations — the mines, smelters, and refineries of Butte, Anaconda, and Great Falls — employed tens of thousands of workers through most of the twentieth century, making it not merely a corporation but the engine of an entire regional economy.

Anaconda's closure of the Butte underground mines and the Berkeley Pit in 1980–83 ended the copper era and left behind the nation's largest Superfund site.

headline: Butte's Founding and the Gold-to-Silver-to-Copper Transition, 1864–1882
summary:
## How Geology Kept Rewriting Butte's Fortunes

Butte's first mining camp was established in 1864, when prospectors following the gold rush into the Montana Territory staked placer claims along Silver Bow Creek in the narrow mountain valley that would become the city's site. The placer gold was largely exhausted by 1867, and most of the early prospectors moved on to richer strikes elsewhere.

What saved Butte was silver. In 1874, William L. Farlin founded the Asteroid Mine — subsequently renamed the Travona — targeting silver veins that ran through the quartz monzonite pluton underlying the hill. His discovery attracted a new wave of prospectors, hard-rock miners equipped for vein mining rather than placer panning, and the population grew rapidly through the 1870s. The Silver Bow district became one of Montana Territory's significant silver producers.

The third and most consequential geological chapter began in 1881, when Marcus Daly recognized that the silver mines were revealing something more valuable at depth: the Butte ore body contained massive deposits of copper sulfide that, in the age of electrical power, were worth more than all the gold and silver combined. Copper's role in wiring the electrical grid, the telephone system, and eventually the motors and generators of industrial civilization made Butte's deposits among the most strategically valuable mineral concentrations on earth.

By 1900, Butte was producing roughly one-quarter of the world's copper supply and had become Montana's largest city with a population approaching 50,000. The Richest Hill on Earth — the phrase attached to the copper-bearing hill rising above the city — had earned its name through three successive chapters of geological fortune.

headline: The War of the Copper Kings — Daly, Clark, and Heinze Battle for Butte
summary:
## When Three Men Fought for the Richest Hill on Earth and Nearly Broke Montana

From the 1880s through the early 1900s, Butte was the center of one of the most spectacular corporate wars in American history, fought among three men whose wealth, ambition, and enmity shaped Montana politics, courts, newspapers, and physical geography for a generation. Marcus Daly, William Andrews Clark, and Fritz Augustus Heinze each controlled significant copper properties and used every available instrument — legal, political, journalistic, and occasionally physical — to dominate the others.

Daly and Clark's war began in the 1880s as a personal rivalry that escalated into a state-level political conflict over the location of the state capital. Daly, who had built the company town of Anaconda, lobbied furiously to make it the capital; Clark, who had spent a fortune acquiring political allies, maneuvered successfully for Helena. Their conflict peaked in the 1899–1900 U.S. Senate fight, when Clark bribed the state legislature to elect him senator — an act exposed in Washington and reversed by the Senate itself.

Heinze entered the conflict in the late 1890s with a legal strategy based on the apex law, filing overlapping mine claims against Amalgamated Copper and corrupting the local judiciary to win verdicts in his favor. His underground crews literally battled Amalgamated's miners in disputed tunnels. In response, Amalgamated in 1903 shut down its Montana operations — throwing 20,000 workers out of employment — until the governor called a special legislative session that removed all judges from cases involving Amalgamated, ending Heinze's legal strategy.

Heinze sold out in 1906; Clark retired from Montana after his Senate term. The war left Anaconda as the unchallenged corporate ruler of Montana's copper economy.

headline: Butte's Irish Identity — How Marcus Daly Planted a County Mayo on the Rockies
summary:
## Deliberate Immigration and the Making of the Most Irish City in America

Butte's extraordinary Irish-American identity — the city claims the largest population of Irish Americans per capita of any city in the United States — was not an accident. It was engineered by Marcus Daly, himself a County Cavan immigrant, who deliberately recruited workers from Ireland and from the Irish immigrant communities of Pennsylvania, Michigan, and Nevada's Comstock Lode to staff his expanding copper operations.

Daly's recruitment was systematic. He sent agents to Ireland, to the coalfields of Pennsylvania, and to the silver mines of Nevada, offering wages above the regional standard and housing in Butte's rapidly expanding neighborhoods. Irish workers — experienced in hard-rock mining from their work in the Cornish and Welsh operations that preceded American mining booms — were disproportionately represented in the skilled underground labor force. By 1900, a substantial fraction of Butte's 47,000 residents were Irish-born or first-generation Irish-American.

The Irish presence shaped Butte's neighborhoods, its pub culture, its political style, and its religious institutions. The city developed one of the largest concentrations of Catholic parishes in the Mountain West, with Irish parish names — St. Patrick's, St. Lawrence O'Toole's — clustered in the working-class mining districts. The Ancient Order of Hibernians and various Irish fraternal organizations became major civic institutions.

St. Patrick's Day in Butte evolved into the city's largest annual celebration, drawing Irish-Americans from across the country to a party that now fills the historic Uptown district. The city's annual "An Ri Ra" Irish festival — meaning "the great noise" — continues to celebrate a heritage planted deliberately by a Cavan immigrant who wanted to recreate something of home in the copper hills of Montana.

headline: The Copper King Mansion — A Victorian Monument to Butte's Gilded Age
summary:
## The House William Andrews Clark Built to Show What Copper Could Buy

Between 1884 and 1888, William Andrews Clark — already one of the wealthiest men in Montana — built a 34-room Victorian mansion in Uptown Butte at an estimated cost of $500,000, the equivalent of roughly $16 million today. The mansion stands at 219 West Granite Street, less than a mile from the mines that generated the fortune that paid for it, and is one of the most elaborate surviving Victorian residences in the Mountain West.

The mansion's construction coincided with the height of the silver-to-copper transition in Butte's mining economy. Clark, who had been accumulating mining claims throughout the 1870s, recognized that copper's future value outweighed silver, and was investing aggressively in deeper copper mines even as the building's ornate woodwork, stained glass, and hand-painted ceramic tiles were being installed. The residence was simultaneously a home and a statement — Clark's architectural declaration that Butte was not a frontier camp but a city capable of sustaining cosmopolitan grandeur.

The mansion features a three-story ballroom, servant quarters, a wine cellar, and elaborate decorative work executed by craftsmen recruited from the East and from Europe. Its Moorish elements, parquet floors, and custom millwork reflect the Victorian eclecticism fashionable among American plutocrats of the 1880s. Clark's art collection — housed partly in the mansion and partly in his New York residences — was eventually donated to what became a major Washington gallery.

The Copper King Mansion operates today as a bed and breakfast, offering tours of its preserved interior and standing as one of Butte's most visited landmarks — a direct architectural relic of the Gilded Age copper economy.

headline: The IWW in Butte — How the Wobblies Made the Richest Hill on Earth a Labor Battleground
summary:
## Radical Unionism in the Shadow of the Anaconda Company

The Industrial Workers of the World arrived in Butte in 1905, two years after the union's founding in Chicago by an alliance of socialist, anarchist, and radical labor leaders including Eugene Debs, Daniel De Leon, and "Big Bill" Haywood. Butte's mining workforce — multilingual, dangerous-employed, and underpaid relative to the wealth it generated — was exactly the constituency the IWW sought to organize. The Western Federation of Miners had been Butte's established union, but after 1905 the IWW operated alongside it, competing for radical workers alienated by what they saw as the WFM's accommodation with capital.

The IWW's vision was industrial unionism — organizing all workers in an industry rather than the skilled-trade model of the American Federation of Labor — and its rhetoric was explicitly revolutionary, calling for the overthrow of the wage system and the creation of worker-controlled industries. In Butte, where the Anaconda Company controlled the newspapers, the politicians, and the hiring system through the "rustling card" that blacklisted union members, the IWW's uncompromising stance attracted workers who had given up on working within the system.

The WFM/IWW split came to a head in the 1914 riots, when IWW-aligned miners dynamited the Miners Union Hall and the established union was effectively destroyed. After 1914, the Anaconda Company withdrew union recognition entirely. The IWW continued organizing through World War I, bringing Frank Little to Butte in 1917 — where he was lynched on August 1. The organization's presence in Butte diminished rapidly after Little's murder and the federal suppression of the IWW under the Espionage Act of 1917.

But the IWW's influence persisted in Butte's labor culture for decades, and the union's history in the copper city remains one of the most intensely studied chapters of American labor history.

headline: Evel Knievel — The Richest Hill's Most Famous Son
summary:
## From the Copper Mines to the Caesars Palace Fountain

Robert Craig Knievel was born on October 17, 1938, in Butte, Montana, in the mining city his grandfather called the Richest Hill on Earth. He was raised by his paternal grandparents, Ignatius and Emma Knievel, after his parents separated when he was a child. At eight years old, a Joie Chitwood auto daredevil show planted the seed of his career ambition. He left Butte High School after his sophomore year and went to work for the Anaconda Mining Company as a diamond drill operator — until he accidentally drove an earth mover into Butte's main power line, knocking out electricity across the city for several hours and getting himself fired.

Knievel tried various ventures in and around Butte — a semi-pro hockey team called the Butte Bombers, an insurance sales business, a Honda motorcycle dealership — before discovering that motorcycle jumping was his calling. His first professional jump was in Indio, California, in January 1966, leaping over two pickup trucks. From there the ambition grew exponentially: more trucks, more buses, eventually the famous attempt at Caesars Palace in Las Vegas on December 31, 1967, in which a crash landing broke his pelvis, both legs, and both wrists, and left him in a coma for 29 days — and made him famous.

Knievel became one of the most recognizable Americans of the 1970s, as famous for his spectacular crashes as for his successes. The Snake River Canyon jump in 1974, the Wembley Stadium crowd of 90,000, the white-leather jumpsuit, and the sky-blue Harley-Davidson Screamin' Eagle all entered popular iconography. He died on November 30, 2007, and was buried in Mountain View Cemetery in Butte, following a funeral at the city's civic center attended by thousands.

headline: Marcus Daly — The Irish Immigrant Who Built Butte's Copper Empire
summary:
## From County Mayo to the Richest Mine in the World

Marcus Daly was born on December 5, 1841, in Ballyjamesduff, County Cavan, Ireland, one of eleven children of a farming family. He emigrated to the United States at age 15, worked in the California gold fields as a young man, and acquired expertise in hard-rock mining that carried him through Nevada's Comstock Lode to Utah's Emma Mine before a Salt Lake City banking family, the Walker Brothers, sent him north to evaluate silver properties in Butte, Montana.

In 1876, Daly arrived in Butte to manage the Walker Brothers' Alice Mine in the suburb of Walkerville. While working the silver veins, he noticed something more valuable below: an extraordinarily rich copper seam. In 1881, Daly bought a small mine called the Anaconda on a hunch, sold his interest in the Alice, and persuaded San Francisco mining magnate George Hearst to finance a quarter-share of the new company without ever visiting the site. When the Anaconda proved to contain one of the richest copper deposits in the history of mining, Daly became one of the wealthiest men in the United States.

Daly built a smelter at the company town of Anaconda — 26 miles from Butte — connected by a railroad he constructed to carry ore from the mines to the furnaces. He recruited thousands of workers from Ireland, deliberately concentrating Irish immigrants in Butte and giving the city the largest Irish-American population per capita in the United States, a cultural identity it retains today.

His rivalry with fellow copper king William A. Clark — culminating in Clark's attempt to move the state capital to Helena and Daly's furious counter-campaign for Anaconda — defined Montana politics for a generation. Daly died on November 12, 1900.

headline: William Andrews Clark — Copper King, Senator, and Art Collector of Questionable Ethics
summary:
## The Man Who Said 'I Never Bought a Man Who Wasn't for Sale'

William Andrews Clark was born on January 8, 1839, in Connellsville, Pennsylvania, and arrived in Montana Territory in 1863 to work gold claims near Bannack. Within two years he recognized that banking, merchandising, and eventually mining capital offered better returns than digging. He parlayed $1,500 in gold into control of several Butte silver mines by the 1870s and pivoted aggressively to copper as electrical power created explosive new demand for the metal.

By 1900, Clark was worth an estimated $50 million — equivalent to roughly $1.6 billion today — controlling copper mines, banks, newspapers across Montana, sugar plantations in California, and 1,100 miles of railroad that became part of the Union Pacific. Between 1884 and 1888, he built the Copper King Mansion in Butte at a cost of approximately $500,000, a 34-room Victorian landmark that still stands and operates as a bed and breakfast.

Clark's political career was as audacious as his business dealings. He tried twice to win election to the U.S. Senate — the first attempt, in 1899, ended when the Senate refused to seat him after determining he had bribed the Montana legislature. He publicly responded: "I never bought a man who wasn't for sale." His second attempt succeeded in 1901, and he served one Senate term.

Clark built Columbia Gardens amusement park in Butte in 1899 and offered free rides to miners and their families — a paternalistic gesture from a man who also ruthlessly suppressed union organizing. He died in New York City on March 2, 1925. His art collection, donated to the Corcoran Gallery, formed the nucleus of what became one of Washington's premier cultural institutions.

headline: Mary MacLane — The Wild Woman of Butte Who Invented Confessional Writing
summary:
## A Teenage Feminist Who Shocked a Nation from the Rimrock City

Mary MacLane was born in Winnipeg, Manitoba, on May 1, 1881, but her family moved to Butte when she was a child, and the mining city — raw, masculine, industrial, and geographically isolated — became the landscape against which she defined herself in opposition. She attended school in Butte and began writing for the school paper in 1897. At 19, she sent a manuscript to a Boston publisher.

The book — *The Story of Mary MacLane* (1902) — was a sensational explosion. Written in the first person with unfiltered candor about its author's desires, ambitions, loneliness, intellectual hunger, and contempt for Butte's provincial social world, the memoir sold over 100,000 copies in its first month and became one of the most discussed books of the decade. H.L. Mencken called MacLane "the Butte Bashkirtseff" — comparing her to the Russian diarist Marie Bashkirtseff, another young woman who had scandalized polite society with frank self-revelation.

MacLane wrote two subsequent memoirs and numerous essays, left Butte for extended periods in Chicago and New York, and in 1918 wrote and starred in a silent film called *Men Who Have Made Love to Me*, based on an earlier essay. She was openly bisexual at a time when such frankness was essentially unprecedented in American public life and described her attraction to women with the same unguarded specificity she brought to everything else.

She returned to Butte periodically throughout her life and died in Chicago on August 6, 1929, at 48. Literary historians credit her as a founding figure of the confessional memoir — a genre that now dominates American nonfiction.

headline: Dashiell Hammett — The Pinkerton Man Who Took Butte's Darkness to Paperback
summary:
## The Detective Who Watched the Copper Wars and Wrote *The Maltese Falcon*

Samuel Dashiell Hammett was born in St. Mary's County, Maryland, on May 27, 1894, and spent much of his early adult life as an operative for the Pinkerton National Detective Agency. In the late 1910s and early 1920s, Hammett said that Pinkerton assignments took him to Butte, Montana, during the height of the labor wars between the Anaconda Copper Mining Company and the Industrial Workers of the World. The copper company regularly employed Pinkerton operatives to surveil union organizers, gather intelligence on strikes, and intimidate workers.

Whether or not Hammett's Butte assignment actually occurred — some biographers have questioned the details — he always maintained that working as a Pinkerton in the mining West was the experience that shaped his fiction. The atmosphere he described: a company-owned city, corrupt police, vigilante violence against union men, the casual brutality of industrial capitalism — is precisely the environment depicted in the Continental Op stories and in *Red Harvest* (1929), his first novel, which is set in a fictional Montana mining city called Personville (known to its inhabitants as Poisonville).

Hammett went on to write *The Dain Curse* (1929), *The Maltese Falcon* (1930), and *The Thin Man* (1934), creating the hard-boiled detective genre and inventing the template for American crime fiction. Sam Spade, the detective of *The Maltese Falcon*, embodied the moral ambiguity of a man who works in a corrupt system without becoming fully corrupt himself — a sensibility that traces directly to the Butte copper wars.

Hammett died on January 10, 1961, in New York City.

headline: Burton K. Wheeler — Butte's Progressive Senator Who Defied Three Presidents
summary:
## The Montana Senator Who Took on Harding, Coolidge, and Roosevelt

Burton Kendall Wheeler was born in Hudson, Massachusetts, on February 27, 1882, and arrived in Butte in 1905 after graduating from the University of Michigan Law School, initially intending to stay only temporarily. The raw energy of the copper city, the intense labor politics, and the opportunities for a young lawyer committed to progressive causes persuaded him to make Butte his home.

Wheeler served as a U.S. District Attorney for Montana from 1913 to 1918, where he made his reputation investigating political corruption and standing against the Anaconda Company's domination of the state. When IWW organizer Frank Little was lynched in Butte in 1917, Wheeler — then the federal prosecutor — concluded that he lacked sufficient evidence to indict, but his refusal to simply rubber-stamp the copper company's version of events made him a hero to Butte's labor movement.

Elected to the U.S. Senate in 1922, Wheeler launched investigations that exposed the Teapot Dome oil scandal under the Harding administration, helping bring down the corrupt Interior Secretary Albert Fall. He ran for vice president on Robert La Follette's Progressive Party ticket in 1924 and remained one of the Senate's most combative progressives through the 1930s. In 1937, he led the successful opposition to Franklin Roosevelt's Supreme Court "packing" plan — arguably Wheeler's most consequential act, preserving the court's independence at a critical constitutional moment.

Wheeler served four Senate terms until 1947, when his increasingly isolationist foreign policy positions had eroded his Montana base. He died in Washington, D.C., on January 6, 1975.

headline: Ella J. Knowles Haskell — Montana's First Female Attorney and Copper City Legal Pioneer
summary:
## The Woman Who Made Montana Law Bend Its Rules to Admit Her

Ella J. Knowles was born in Northwood, New Hampshire, on July 31, 1860, and graduated from Bates College in Maine before teaching school in New Hampshire and later in Montana, to which she moved in 1888. She read law and applied to be admitted to the Montana bar in 1889, the same year Montana achieved statehood. There was one problem: Montana law specified that attorneys must be "male citizens."

Knowles petitioned the first Montana State Legislature to amend the law. Impressed by her legal credentials and her argument, the legislature passed a bill allowing women to practice law in Montana in 1889, and she was admitted to the bar the same year — the first woman to practice law in the state's history. She established her practice in Butte, where the mining boom created constant demand for legal services in property disputes, contracts, labor relations, and corporate law.

In 1892, Knowles ran for Montana Attorney General on the Populist Party ticket, becoming the first woman nominated for statewide office in Montana. She lost narrowly to her Republican opponent, Henri Haskell — whom she subsequently married in 1895, becoming Ella Knowles Haskell. Their marriage did not survive long, but her name became permanently linked to both her legal career and her political trailblazing.

Knowles Haskell continued to practice law in Butte and Helena for years, taking cases that advanced labor rights and women's legal standing in Montana. She died on January 27, 1911, in Butte, at 50. The Montana Supreme Court building has honored her memory as a founding figure in the state's legal history.

headline: Martha Raye — Butte's Brassy Entertainer and Honorary Green Beret
summary:
## Born on St. Patrick's Day in a Mining City, Buried with Soldiers

Martha Raye was born Margarett Theresa Yvonne Reed on August 27, 1916, in Butte, Montana, to vaudeville performers who happened to be performing in the city when she arrived. Her parents were already on the circuit, and she literally joined the act before she could walk, carried on stage in her first days. Butte — Irish, Catholic, working-class, gregarious — left its mark on her humor and her character even as the family moved constantly.

Raye became a major radio, film, and television star in the 1930s through the 1950s, known for physical comedy, a brash delivery style, and an extraordinary singing voice. She appeared in more than 40 films, was a regular on television variety programs, and was one of the highest-paid performers in the country during her prime years. But what defined her legacy was not entertainment but service: beginning in World War II, she began performing for American troops in war zones, a commitment that extended through the Korean War and most remarkably through three tours of Vietnam.

In Vietnam, Raye spent months at a time in the field with combat units, sometimes performing in active combat zones and reportedly treating wounded soldiers as an honorary nurse. The Special Forces community adopted her as "Colonel Maggie" — she was made an honorary colonel — and when she died on October 19, 1994, President Clinton awarded her the Presidential Medal of Freedom. At her request, she was buried at Fort Bragg, North Carolina, in the Special Forces cemetery — the only woman interred there.

headline: F. Augustus Heinze — The Copper King Who Played the Courts Like a Mine
summary:
## The Brooklyn Lawyer Who Outmaneuvered Standard Oil in Butte

Friedrich Augustus Heinze was born on December 5, 1869, in Brooklyn, New York, into a prosperous German-American family. He graduated from the Columbia School of Mines in 1889 and arrived in Butte the same year to work as a mining engineer. Within a few years, he had bought his own mines and begun a legal campaign against the Anaconda Copper Mining Company that was as audacious as anything Daly or Clark had attempted.

Heinze's weapon was the apex law — a doctrine of American mining law holding that a miner whose claim "apexed" (touched the surface) at the top of a vein could follow that vein wherever it went underground, including into neighboring claims. In Butte's geologically complex ore body, where veins intersected and overlapped at depth, the apex doctrine was a litigation goldmine. Heinze hired sympathetic judges, filed apex claims against Amalgamated Copper (the Rockefeller/Daly company), and extracted legal settlements worth millions while his underground crews raced to extract ore that courts had awarded him.

The war between Heinze and Amalgamated involved actual underground battles — miners fighting each other with tools and dynamite in disputed tunnels, companies pumping toxic gases through shared shafts, and judges issuing contradictory injunctions that shut entire mines. In 1907, Heinze sold his Butte operations to Amalgamated for $10.5 million — about $340 million in today's terms — and moved to New York, where his financial schemes (blamed by some for contributing to the Panic of 1907) led to the collapse of his Knickerbocker Trust.

Heinze died in Saratoga, New York, on November 4, 1914, at 44.

headline: Judy Martz — Olympic Speed Skater Who Became Governor of Montana
summary:
## From the Ice to the State Capitol

Judy Morstein Martz was born on July 28, 1943, in Big Timber, Montana, but was raised in Butte and attended schools there during the height of the copper city's postwar life. She was a gifted athlete who competed in speed skating, qualifying for the 1964 Olympic Games in Innsbruck, Austria, as a member of the United States Olympic speed skating team. The Olympic experience — representing Montana and America in an international sporting context — remained central to her public identity throughout her political career.

After her athletic career, Martz entered business in Butte and later moved into Republican politics in Silver Bow County, winning a seat in the Montana House of Representatives in 1999 as part of the Republican legislative majority. The same year, she was elected Lieutenant Governor of Montana on a ticket with Governor Marc Racicot. When Racicot stepped down in 2001 to chair the Republican National Committee, Martz became Montana's 20th governor — and the first woman to hold that office.

Martz served as governor from 2001 to 2005, focusing on economic development, tax reduction, and resource extraction policies. Her tenure was complicated by the environmental legacy of the Berkeley Pit and Butte's Superfund designation, issues that placed her in frequent tension with federal regulators and environmental advocates.

She did not seek re-election in 2004. Butte's claim on her was real and consistent: the copper city's work ethic, the Olympic competitor's discipline, and the Republican politics of a resource-dependent western state all converged in her career.

headline: The Five Great Falls: Lewis and Clark's Epic Portage of 1805
summary:
## Eighteen Miles of Struggle at the Heart of the Continent

In late May 1805, the Corps of Discovery reached the roar of falling water and knew they had found the Great Falls of the Missouri that Native informants had described. What they had not been told was that the falls were not one cascade but five successive cataracts spanning eighteen miles of canyon—a revelation that turned a planned half-day portage into a brutal three-week ordeal.

Meriwether Lewis first viewed the largest fall on June 13, 1805, scrambling to the top of a rocky bluff above the Missouri to gaze at what he called "the grandest sight I ever beheld." The main fall dropped roughly eighty feet and was flanked by additional cascades at Black Eagle, Crooked Falls, Rainbow Falls, and Colter Falls. The Corps built crude wagons from cottonwood and hauled their heavy dugout canoes and equipment over cactus-studded prairie in temperatures that swung from searing heat to violent hailstorms.

The men wore out their moccasins, suffered cactus wounds to their feet, and endured relentless grizzly bear encounters. Captain Clark mapped the portage route meticulously, and those maps became crucial documents of western geography. Sacagawea, gravely ill during the portage, recovered enough to continue west. The Corps cached their largest boat—an iron-framed vessel they had hauled from Illinois—after it failed to hold pitch in the dry Montana air.

The Great Falls Portage was designated a National Historic Landmark in 1966. The Lewis and Clark National Historic Trail Interpretive Center, built on a bluff above the Missouri in Great Falls, preserves the story and overlooks the same cottonwood-lined river the Corps navigated more than two centuries ago.

headline: Paris Gibson Founds Great Falls: A City Planned from Scratch in 1883
summary:
## When a Maine Businessman Staked a City on Hydroelectric Power

In the summer of 1880, Paris Gibson stood at the edge of the Missouri River and looked at the five cascades that gave the site its name. While others saw scenery, Gibson saw shaft horsepower. Convincing his friend James J. Hill to back a townsite company and extend his rail line through the new city, Gibson spent the next three years assembling land, recruiting settlers, and designing a municipal grid of exceptional clarity.

Great Falls was formally incorporated in 1883 with Gibson as its first mayor. The city's street plan—wide numbered avenues running east-west, lettered streets north-south—was laid out with the precision of an engineer's compass, making the growing frontier town unusually navigable. Gibson actively recruited immigrant workers and marketed the city to eastern investors as a coming industrial hub.

The timing proved right. The Great Northern Railway's main line reached Great Falls in 1887, linking it to Helena, Butte, and eventually the Pacific Coast. The railroad brought the Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company, which erected its first smelter in Black Eagle in 1890. Within a decade of incorporation, Great Falls had a smelter, multiple dams under construction, and a population in the thousands.

Gibson remained the city's civic patriarch, serving in the U.S. Senate from 1901 to 1905 and living in Great Falls until his death in 1920. The city he laid out with a ruler and a vision endures today as Montana's third-largest, its streets still running on the grid he drew.

headline: The Black Eagle Smelter: How Great Falls Became the Electric City
summary:
## Copper, Steam, and the 506-Foot Stack That Defined a Skyline

In 1890, the Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company chose a bluff overlooking the Missouri River just north of Great Falls to build Montana's largest smelter. The location was no accident: the Great Falls dams could supply cheap hydroelectric power, and the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads could carry copper ore from Butte and refined wire to eastern markets.

By 1908, the construction of the 506-foot smokestacks had begun—the tallest structure in Montana at the time, visible for thirty miles across the high plains. The operation refined copper into the wire that helped electrify American cities, supplied essential materials in both World Wars, and at its peak employed more than four thousand workers in the smelter and the adjacent community of Black Eagle.

The Anaconda Copper Mining Company absorbed the Black Eagle operation from Boston and Montana in 1915. Workers came from Croatia, Italy, Norway, Greece, and a dozen other nations, building a multicultural neighborhood of bars, churches, and company housing in the shadow of the stack. The Black Eagle Dam, completed in 1891, fed power directly to the smelter, and it was this connection that earned Great Falls the enduring nickname "the Electric City."

The smelter closed in 1980 amid labor strikes and falling copper prices. On September 19, 1981, six hundred pounds of dynamite brought the great stack down in thirty seconds—an event that drew thousands to the riverbank to watch a century of industrial history collapse into rubble. The Anaconda superfund cleanup continues on the former 405-acre site today.

headline: Ryan Dam and the Hydroelectric Chain That Powered a City
summary:
## Building the Missouri's Power Staircase, One Dam at a Time

The Missouri River drops more than five hundred feet as it tumbles through the canyon at Great Falls, a hydrological gift that Paris Gibson recognized in 1880 and that engineers spent the following four decades harnessing. The result was a cascade of five dams—Black Eagle, Rainbow, Cochrane, Ryan, and Morony—that made Great Falls one of the most intensively hydroelectrified stretches of river in the American West.

Ryan Dam, built on the largest of the five falls and completed in 1915, was the crown of the project. At 1,336 feet long and 61 feet high, with a six-unit powerhouse on the left bank, it occupied what Lewis and Clark had called "Grand Fall"—the same 87-foot cascade they had marveled at in June 1805. The dam's reservoir stretches seven miles upstream, and its six turbines still generate 72 megawatts. Contemporary newspapers called it likely the largest and most modern hydroelectric plant west of the Mississippi.

Black Eagle Dam had come first, completed in 1891 primarily to supply direct current to the Boston and Montana smelter. Rainbow Dam followed in 1910. Together, the five dams formed a continuous power staircase that supported not just the copper industry but the city's growing residential and commercial grid. Montana Power Company, a direct descendant of the Anaconda smelter's power operations, managed the system through most of the twentieth century.

Today the dams remain operational under NorthWestern Corporation. Ryan Dam sits within sight of a popular island picnic area where visitors can see—just barely—the remnant cascades that once made this one of the most dramatic river sights on the continent.

headline: Malmstrom Air Force Base: From Lend-Lease Hub to ICBM Fortress
summary:
## How Great Falls Became America's First Minuteman Missile Command

In June 1942, with war raging in Europe and the Pacific, construction began on a U.S. Army Air Corps base six miles east of Great Falls. The facility's initial mission was to serve as the American terminus of the Northwest Staging Route—the air corridor through Canada and Alaska by which thousands of combat aircraft were transferred to the Soviet Union under the Lend-Lease program. Over twenty-one months, approximately 8,000 aircraft passed through Great Falls bound for Soviet pilots on the Eastern Front.

After World War II the base evolved through multiple missions: Berlin Airlift crew training, Cold War air defense, and Strategic Air Command operations. On October 1, 1955, it was renamed Malmstrom Air Force Base in honor of Colonel Einar Malmstrom, the popular vice wing commander who had died in a 1954 training crash near the base.

The most consequential transformation came in 1959, when the Air Force Ballistic Missile Committee selected Malmstrom to host the nation's first Minuteman ICBM complex. Construction of launch silos began across the central Montana plains in March 1961. By July 1962 the first Minuteman I missile was operational at Launch Facility Alpha-09—making Great Falls the nucleus of America's ground-based nuclear deterrent.

Today the 341st Missile Wing at Malmstrom controls 150 Minuteman III ICBMs scattered across a 13,800-square-mile area of Montana prairie. The base remains the largest of three Minuteman wings in the country and Great Falls' largest employer, a Cold War institution that shows no sign of standing down.

headline: The Blackfeet and the Missouri: Ancient Ties to the Falls Country
summary:
## Long Before the Dams, the Niitsitapi Called This River Home

For hundreds of years before Paris Gibson drew his grid or the Great Northern Railroad laid its tracks, the Great Falls of the Missouri River fell through the territory of the Niitsitapi—the Blackfoot Confederacy. The Piegan Blackfeet (Amsskáápipikani) ranged across the high plains north of the Missouri, following the buffalo herds that sustained their culture and economy.

The falls themselves were a landmark in Blackfoot oral geography, known as a place of power and spiritual significance. Lewis and Clark's 1805 journals document encounters with Blackfoot people along the Marias River, less than fifty miles north of the falls, and the expedition's tense confrontation with a Piegan raiding party on the return journey in 1806 left one of the few violent episodes of the entire two-year trek.

As the fur trade era gave way to the reservation era, Blackfoot territory contracted sharply. A series of treaties—culminating in the establishment of the Blackfeet Reservation in northern Montana in 1855—confined the Niitsitapi to land east and north of the present-day Rocky Mountain Front. The Lame Bull Treaty of 1855 set the southern boundary of the Blackfeet Reservation and effectively ceded the falls country to the United States.

The Blackfeet Nation today, headquartered in Browning, Montana, maintains cultural and historical ties to the Great Falls region. The Lewis and Clark Interpretive Center in Great Falls acknowledges the Blackfoot presence that pre-dated every monument, dam, and museum in the city by centuries.

headline: The Great Northern Railway Arrives: Great Falls Joins the Continental Grid
summary:
## James J. Hill's Iron Road and the Making of a Commercial Hub

When Paris Gibson sought a partner to transform his hydroelectric dream into a working city, he turned to James J. Hill—the Canadian-born "Empire Builder" who was then laying track westward across the northern Great Plains toward the Pacific. Their alliance proved decisive. By 1887, Hill's rail lines had connected Great Falls to Helena and Butte, giving the young city its economic lifelines.

The Great Northern Railway's main transcontinental line, completed in 1893, passed north of Great Falls through Havre. But a branch line provided direct service to the city, and the combination of affordable freight rates and Hill's active promotion of agriculture and industry along his route transformed Great Falls into the premier trade center of north-central Montana. Grain from the vast wheat farms of the highline flowed through Great Falls elevators. Cattle from the open range came through Great Falls stockyards.

Hill's personal connection to Gibson—they had known each other during Gibson's Minnesota years—made Great Falls something of a favored project. Hill invested in the Great Falls Water Power and Townsite Company alongside Gibson and lobbied aggressively for federal land policies that encouraged agricultural settlement on the Montana plains.

The legacy of the Great Northern is visible today in the grain elevators, stockyards district, and the street layout of Great Falls' commercial core. The railroad that Hill built without government land grants—a rare achievement in the land-grant era—left its structural imprint on the city as surely as any single building.

headline: C. M. Russell Museum: Building a Legacy Around a Log Cabin Studio
summary:
## How Great Falls Became the Custodian of the Cowboy Artist's World

Charles Marion Russell built his log-cabin studio on Fourth Street North in Great Falls in 1903, and for the next twenty-three years it was the engine of his creative life. Twelve feet wide and twenty feet deep, with a north-facing skylight and walls covered in Native American artifacts, the studio was his sanctuary—off-limits to business talk, open to stories, laughter, and the company of old cowboys who dropped by to chew tobacco and remember the open range.

When Russell died in 1926, his wife Nancy worked to preserve the studio and the adjoining home, known as "Trail's End," understanding that the physical setting was as much a part of the Russell legacy as the paintings. The studio became a designated historic landmark. Over the following decades, Great Falls' civic leadership and cultural community organized around the goal of building a world-class museum worthy of Russell's stature.

The C. M. Russell Museum complex today occupies the block adjacent to the original studio and home. It holds the largest collection of Russell's original artwork in the world—paintings, bronze sculptures, illustrated letters, and personal effects—as well as archival materials documenting Montana's frontier era. The annual C. M. Russell Auction, held each March in Great Falls, is one of the premier Western art sales in the country.

The museum transformed Great Falls into a pilgrimage destination for Western art lovers and historians. What began as one man's log cabin studio became the anchor of a cultural identity that Great Falls has carried proudly into the twenty-first century.

headline: Gore Field and the Lend-Lease Air Bridge: Great Falls Goes to War
summary:
## How a Montana City Became the Pivot Point of an Unlikely Alliance

In the months after Pearl Harbor, Great Falls became the American nerve center of one of World War II's most consequential logistics operations. The Northwest Staging Route—a chain of airfields threading through Canada and Alaska to Fairbanks—was the conduit through which the United States delivered aircraft to the Soviet Union under the Lend-Lease program. Gore Field, on the west side of Great Falls, was where the pipeline began.

Bell P-39 Airacobras, Douglas C-47 Skytrains, North American B-25 Mitchells, and Douglas A-20 Havocs arrived at Gore Field by rail, were assembled, and were flown by American pilots to Fairbanks, where Soviet pilots took over for the final leg to Siberia. Over twenty-one months, approximately 8,000 aircraft made the journey. The operation's scale was staggering: 1,717,712 pounds of cargo—aircraft parts, explosives, medical supplies—were shipped through Great Falls to the Soviet Union.

Great Falls Army Air Base, just east of the city (later Malmstrom), handled the training and logistics support. The two bases together made Great Falls perhaps the most important single point in the entire Lend-Lease network. Historians have also noted that the operation's diplomatic channel was used to transfer sensitive documents and personnel in ways that remain subjects of ongoing historical inquiry.

The Lend-Lease operation ended in September 1945. Great Falls emerged from the war with two substantial military installations, a workforce accustomed to industrial production, and a strategic importance that would carry into the Cold War era.

headline: The Boston and Montana Smelter at Black Eagle: Wiring the World
summary:
## The Industrial Colossus That Made Great Falls the Electric City

In 1890, two years before Montana's statehood infrastructure had fully settled, the Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company planted a smelter on a basalt bluff overlooking Black Eagle Dam north of Great Falls. It was the first major industrial facility in the city, and it would operate—under various corporate owners—for nearly ninety years.

The site was chosen for reasons that Paris Gibson had anticipated a decade earlier: the Missouri River dams could supply direct current electricity at costs unavailable anywhere else in the region. Black Eagle Dam, completed in 1891, ran power cables directly to the smelter's electric furnaces. The smelter refined raw copper ore from Butte into the wire that would help electrify American homes, power telegraph networks, and supply the munitions industry in both World Wars.

Ammalgamated Copper—later the Anaconda Copper Mining Company—absorbed the Boston and Montana operation by 1915. The company built the iconic 506-foot smokestack in 1908, and the community of Black Eagle grew up around it: company housing, a company school, six bars open round-the-clock. At its World War II peak, more than 4,000 workers labored on the hill.

The Great Falls smelter was not a smelter in its final decades but a refinery and wire mill—processing copper that had been smelted in Anaconda into finished industrial products. When the wire mill closed on July 1, 1980, and the stack came down in September 1981, it marked the end of the industrial era that had made Great Falls "the Electric City." The superfund cleanup of the former 405-acre site continues today.

headline: Charles M. Russell — The Cowboy Artist of Great Falls
summary:
## Painting the West from a Studio on Fourth Street North

Charles Marion Russell arrived in Montana as a teenager in 1880, drawn by the romance of the open range. He worked as a wrangler and night herder in the Judith Basin before settling permanently in Great Falls in 1897, where he built the log-cabin studio on Fourth Street North that became his workshop and legend for nearly three decades.

Russell produced more than 2,000 oil and watercolor paintings, thousands of drawings, and scores of bronze sculptures—all celebrating the cowboys, Native peoples, and landscapes of the vanishing frontier West. His works combined technical mastery with deep affection for his subjects; he had lived alongside Blackfoot people and understood their ways in ways few white artists of his era did. His advocacy helped pass the legislation that created the Rocky Boy Reservation in 1916.

His wife Nancy managed his career with fierce effectiveness, negotiating sales, organizing exhibitions, and eventually building his international reputation with shows in New York, London, and beyond. The couple's 1926 home on Fourth Street, the "Trail's End" house, still stands adjacent to the C. M. Russell Museum complex, which today holds the world's largest collection of his original works.

Russell died in Great Falls on October 24, 1926. His funeral procession drew the entire city; schoolchildren were released from class to line the streets. The day the cowboy artist died, Great Falls lost one of its defining voices—and gained an enduring monument to Western American art.

headline: Paris Gibson — Visionary Founder Who Built Great Falls on a Grid
summary:
## From a Maine Farm to the Falls of the Missouri

Paris Gibson was born in Brownfield, Maine, on July 1, 1830, and graduated from Bowdoin College in 1851. After business failures in Minnesota, he traveled west in 1880 and stood at the edge of the Great Falls of the Missouri River. He saw not a scenic wonder but a power source capable of driving an industrial city, and spent the next decade making that vision real.

Gibson convinced his friend, railroad magnate James J. Hill, to extend his rail lines through the new townsite. In 1883 the city of Great Falls was incorporated, with Gibson serving as its first mayor. He designed the town along a precise grid—wide streets, logical blocks—that still defines the city's navigable layout today. By 1887, Hill's rails linked Great Falls to Helena and Butte, anchoring its position as the commercial center of north-central Montana.

Gibson's ambitions extended beyond commerce. He was a delegate to Montana's constitutional convention in 1889 and was appointed to fill a U.S. Senate vacancy in 1901, subsequently elected to serve a full term, representing Montana as a Democrat until 1905. Even after leaving Washington he remained in Great Falls, spending his final years there until his death on December 16, 1920.

The city he planned from bare prairie endures as a testament to one man's capacity to see potential where others saw only wilderness. His grave at Highland Cemetery lies within sight of the Missouri River that first captured his imagination.

headline: Mike Mansfield — From the Mines of Butte to the Senate Floor
summary:
## A Great Falls Boyhood That Shaped America's Longest Senate Leader

Mike Mansfield was born in New York City on March 16, 1903, to Irish immigrant parents. When his mother died and his father was unable to care for him, young Mike and his siblings moved to Great Falls, Montana, around 1910, to be raised by an aunt and uncle. The Missouri River town shaped his character—its working-class ethos, its ethnic immigrant neighborhoods, its blue-collar directness.

At fourteen he lied about his age and enlisted in the Navy, then served in the Army and the Marines, which sent him to the Philippines and China. Those years kindled a lifelong fascination with Asia that would define his statecraft. Returning to Montana, he worked as a mucker in the copper mines of Butte before earning a college degree and eventually a master's in history. He joined the faculty at the University of Montana in Missoula.

Elected to the U.S. House in 1942 and the Senate in 1952, Mansfield rose to become Senate Majority Leader in 1961—a position he held for sixteen years, the longest tenure in Senate history. He guided the Civil Rights Act of 1964 through a historic filibuster and advised presidents on foreign policy from Roosevelt through Ford.

After leaving the Senate in 1977, President Carter appointed him U.S. Ambassador to Japan, where he served until 1988. Mansfield died on October 5, 2001, and was buried at Arlington Cemetery. His tombstone reads simply: *PVT, US Marine Corps*—the rank he prized above all others.

headline: Einar Malmstrom — The Pilot Great Falls Immortalized in Steel and Concrete
summary:
## A Short Tenure That Left a Permanent Name on Montana's Skyline

Colonel Einar Axel Malmstrom was born in 1907 and rose through the ranks of the U.S. Army Air Forces to become one of the more decorated combat pilots of World War II. Flying a fighter escort mission over Europe, he was shot down on his 58th combat sortie and spent much of the war as a prisoner at Stalag Luft I South Compound in Barth, Germany, eventually becoming the senior American commander at the camp.

After liberation and repatriation, Malmstrom returned to active duty. In 1954 he was assigned as vice wing commander at Great Falls Air Force Base—a Cold War hub that had grown from a wartime B-17 training field into a major strategic installation. His tenure was brief but impactful: he was admired by the local community for his accessibility, his energy, and his genuine interest in the people of Great Falls.

On August 21, 1954, Malmstrom died when the T-33 Shooting Star trainer he was piloting crashed approximately one mile west of the Great Falls Municipal Airport. The loss struck the city hard. Local residents and civic leaders launched a campaign to rename the base in his honor. On October 1, 1955, Great Falls Air Force Base was officially redesignated Malmstrom Air Force Base.

The base went on to become the nation's first Minuteman ICBM installation in 1962 and remains today one of three active Minuteman III missile wings. Every time the name appears on a map, a shipping label, or a news dispatch, it carries the memory of a pilot Great Falls refused to forget.

headline: A. B. Guthrie Jr. — Pulitzer Laureate of the Big Sky
summary:
## Writing the West from the Shadow of the Rocky Mountain Front

Alfred Bertram "Bud" Guthrie Jr. was born in Indiana in 1901 but arrived in Montana at six months old when his father became the first principal of Choteau's high school. He grew up exploring the vast grasslands and mountain foothills that would later fill his fiction with aching authenticity. After earning a journalism degree from the University of Montana and spending two decades as a newspaper editor in Kentucky, he returned to Montana—splitting his time between Choteau and Great Falls.

His 1947 novel *The Big Sky* redefined the mountain-man Western, stripping away romanticism and replacing it with hard beauty and harder choices. The follow-up, *The Way West* (1949), won the Pulitzer Prize for Fiction in 1950, tracing the brutal realities of the Oregon Trail emigration. His screenplay for *Shane* (1953) earned an Academy Award nomination and proved his storytelling translated seamlessly to the screen.

Guthrie used Great Falls as his city base—libraries, publishers, contacts—while the solitude of his Choteau cabin and his land near the mountain front fed the landscapes of his imagination. He became an outspoken environmentalist in later decades, donating eighty acres to the Nature Conservancy's Pine Butte Preserve.

He died at his home west of Choteau on April 26, 1991, having done more than any other writer to capture Montana's territorial-era spirit on the page. The nickname he gave the state—"The Big Sky Country"—became official state shorthand, printed on every license plate in Montana.

headline: Monte Dolack — Great Falls-Born Artist of the Natural World
summary:
## Posters, Prints, and a Deep Love for Western Wildlife

Monte Dolack was born in Great Falls, Montana, in 1950 and found his artistic calling early, eventually establishing himself as one of the most widely recognized visual artists in the American West. His signature style—lush, saturated, often humorous depictions of wildlife in improbable situations—earned him national and international recognition from galleries, conservation organizations, and collectors.

Dolack studied art formally and built a prolific studio practice, producing posters, prints, and paintings that placed wild animals in juxtaposition with human environments in ways that managed to be simultaneously whimsical and pointed. His work has appeared in the Smithsonian and in major museum collections, and his posters for Montana conservation campaigns and cultural events became iconic within the state.

His connection to Great Falls remained deep throughout his life. In December 2001, he created a major new work—a 23-by-34-inch piece depicting Lewis and Clark and the Corps of Discovery at the White Cliffs of the Missouri—and donated it to the Lewis and Clark Interpretive Center near Black Eagle Dam, just outside the city where he was born. The image was used in fundraising campaigns for the center, tying his art directly to Great Falls' most celebrated stretch of river.

Dolack's legacy as a Great Falls native who built his art around the landscapes and wild creatures of the Northern Rockies places him in the lineage of C. M. Russell—a lineage of artists who found in Montana's natural world both subject matter and spiritual sustenance.

headline: Myrna Loy — Hollywood's "Perfect Wife" Who Began in the Big Sky
summary:
## From Helena's Radersburg Valley to the Silver Screen

Myrna Adele Williams was born on August 2, 1905, in Helena, Montana, though her family roots extended into the farming communities south of the city. She spent her early years in rural Radersburg before her family relocated to Helena proper, and later moved to California after her father's death in 1918. As she rose to stardom, Montana's wide horizons remained part of her self-understanding.

Renaming herself Myrna Loy for the screen, she appeared in dozens of silent films before finding her defining persona in the sound era. Her chemistry with William Powell in *The Thin Man* (1934) created one of Hollywood's most beloved screen couples. She was nominated for no competitive Academy Awards during her peak years—a snub historians and critics have long found baffling—but received an honorary Oscar in 1991.

Beyond film, Loy was a committed activist who worked for the American Red Cross, UNESCO, and civil rights causes. She was one of the first Hollywood stars to speak out against McCarthyism. She served on the National Committee Against Discrimination in Housing and lobbied Congress for fair housing legislation.

Loy died in New York City on December 14, 1993. The Myrna Loy Center for the Performing Arts in Helena—housed in the former Lewis and Clark County Jail—carries her name as a tribute to the state's most celebrated actress. Her life traced a path from Montana's frontier landscape to the center of twentieth-century American culture.

headline: Jeannette Rankin — Great Falls' Adopted Fighter for Peace and Suffrage
summary:
## The First Woman in Congress Found Her Political Home on the High Plains

Jeannette Rankin was born near Missoula, Montana, in 1880 but her political career and deepest public roots were forged in Great Falls and across Montana's First Congressional District. After earning a degree in biology and working as a social worker in Seattle and New York, she returned to Montana in 1910 to lead the state's suffrage campaign, crisscrossing the plains and the mountains by train and wagon.

In 1916 she was elected to the U.S. House of Representatives—the first woman ever to serve in Congress, four years before women had the constitutional right to vote nationwide. Based in her Great Falls district office during her congressional terms, she tackled labor rights, women's suffrage, and child welfare legislation. She cast one of the fifty votes against U.S. entry into World War I in 1917.

Defeated in a Senate bid and absent from Congress for two decades, she returned in 1940 and on December 8, 1941, cast the lone vote against declaring war on Japan after the attack on Pearl Harbor. The vote ended her congressional career but became the act for which she is most remembered—a principled, solitary stand against the tide.

Rankin spent her later years as an antiwar activist, leading a march of five thousand women against the Vietnam War in Washington in 1968, at age eighty-seven. She died in 1973. A bronze statue of her stands in the U.S. Capitol's National Statuary Hall, representing Montana—the state whose wide-open politics gave a woman a seat in Congress before almost anywhere else on Earth.

headline: William Andrews Clark — Copper King Whose Smelter Electrified Great Falls
summary:
## The Senator Who Helped Wire the World from a Bluff Above the Missouri

William Andrews Clark was born in Pennsylvania in 1839 and made his first fortune in the silver and copper mines beneath Butte Hill. One of Montana's three so-called Copper Kings—alongside Marcus Daly and F. Augustus Heinze—Clark was as much a businessman-politician as a miner, owning newspapers, railroads, banks, and the smelter at Black Eagle that became Great Falls' largest employer.

Clark's Boston and Montana Consolidated Copper and Silver Mining Company erected its first smelter in Black Eagle in 1890, drawn by the hydroelectric power of the Great Falls dams on the Missouri River. By the time the Anaconda company absorbed the operation in 1915, the Black Eagle smelter employed more than a thousand men—immigrants from Croatia, Italy, Norway, Greece, and beyond—and its towering stack had become the visual emblem of the city.

Clark's Senate career was one of the most tainted in Montana history. He openly bribed state legislators to win a Senate seat in 1899, resigned in disgrace when the U.S. Senate moved to expel him, then won re-election in 1901 and served until 1907. His war with Marcus Daly over the state capital location—ultimately won by Helena—consumed millions of dollars and defined Montana's Gilded Age politics.

Despite his infamy, Clark's investment in Great Falls infrastructure was transformative. The smelter he built produced the copper wire that helped electrify American cities and supplied essential materials through two world wars. His legacy in Great Falls is inseparable from the electric city it became.

headline: Wilbur F. Sanders — Helena's First Senator Who Helped Build the Rule of Law
summary:
## The Vigilante Prosecutor Who Became Montana's Constitutional Conscience

Wilbur Fisk Sanders was born in New York State in 1834 and arrived in Montana Territory in 1863 as a young lawyer accompanying his uncle, territorial governor Sidney Edgerton. He reached Bannack at precisely the moment the road agent gang of Henry Plummer was terrorizing the gold camps, and stepped forward to prosecute the murderer George Ives before the miners' court in December 1863—an act of extraordinary courage that established him as the territory's leading legal voice.

Sanders moved with the territorial capital from Bannack to Virginia City and eventually to Helena in the 1870s, where he built a three-story home that still stands. He served as corresponding secretary of the Society of Montana Pioneers, helped found the Montana Bar Association, and for twenty-five years was president of the Montana Historical Society. His law practice included years as regional counsel for the Northern Pacific Railroad.

When Montana achieved statehood in 1889, the newly convened legislature elected Sanders as one of Montana's first two U.S. Senators. He served the short term, from January 1890 to March 1893, bringing Helena's establishment Republican politics to Washington at the moment the state was finding its constitutional footing.

Sanders died in Helena on July 7, 1905, and was interred at Forestvale Cemetery. A bronze statue of him stands in the Montana State Capitol rotunda—silent testimony to a man who helped transform a territory built on gold and chaos into something resembling ordered governance.

headline: Bullhook Bottoms to Havre: How a Railroad Town Got Its Name
summary:
## A Brawl, a Vote, and a Norman Port City

Before there was a Havre, there was Bullhook Bottoms—a rough encampment of tents, tar-paper shacks, and railroad workers gathered at the point where the Great Northern Railway had established its locomotive shops along a creek called Bullhook. The name suited the place. In the early 1890s, it was a frontier settlement in every sense: hard liquor, card games, and the constant sound of hammers driving spikes into the Hi-Line prairie.

When the settlers decided the town needed a proper name, they called a meeting. That first gathering ended in a fistfight. The second was more orderly—though only marginally. The original five homesteaders were declared the only legitimate voters: Gus Decelles, Exzelia James Pepin (nephew of rancher Simon Pepin), Tom McDevitt, Joe Demars, and Charlie Goutchie. Several names were floated, including "France," as a nod to the strong Franco-Canadian contingent among the founders.

Decelles suggested Havre, after his parents' hometown of Le Havre, Normandy, on the northern coast of France. The name—meaning "haven" or "harbor"—won the vote. On September 5, 1893, twenty-six residents voted to formally incorporate the city under that name. The choice reflected a surprisingly cosmopolitan thread running through what was otherwise a raw frontier camp: these were men who had crossed oceans and continents before laying down roots on the Montana plains.

The name Havre stuck and eventually lent the city its nickname—the Crown Jewel of the Hi-Line. A statue of James J. Hill, whose railroad had created the town, stands near the Havre Amtrak station today. Next to it sits Great Northern locomotive No. 2584, preserved in place as a reminder of the industrial force that turned a creek-bottom camp into a city.

headline: Fort Assinniboine: Montana's Largest Military Post, Six Miles from Havre
summary:
## Buffalo Soldiers, Sitting Bull's Shadow, and a Landless People's Refuge

Fort Assinniboine was built in 1879, six miles southwest of what is now Havre, in direct response to the aftermath of the Battle of Little Bighorn. General Phil Sheridan had concluded that a major fort on the Milk River would block any potential incursion south from Canada by Sioux bands loyal to Sitting Bull or by the Nez Perce who had escaped across the border after the Battle of Bear Paw. The Army surveyed the site and began construction that summer.

At its peak, the fort was the largest military installation in Montana—104 buildings spread across a reservation of 704,000 acres, stretching from the Bears Paw Mountains south to the Missouri River. More than 750 officers, enlisted men, and their families were garrisoned there. Among the most significant units stationed at Fort Assinniboine was the 10th Cavalry, the famous Buffalo Soldiers—African-American troops who served under white officers during the Indian Wars. The 10th Cavalry was later mobilized to Cuba during the Spanish-American War, where soldiers supported Theodore Roosevelt's Rough Riders at the Battle of San Juan Hill in 1898.

The Army declared the fort no longer necessary in 1911 and abandoned it. Most of the buildings were razed and hauled away by settlers for lumber. In 1916, Congress authorized the Rocky Boy's Indian Reservation on a portion of the former fort lands—set aside for landless Chippewa and Cree peoples who had been pushed out of their traditional territories and had camped within the fort's reservation for years.

Today, surviving brick structures at the site house the Northern Agricultural Research Center operated by Montana State University. The fort is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. Guided summer tours interpret the frontier military history and the complex story of the Buffalo Soldiers, making Fort Assinniboine one of the most historically layered sites on the Hi-Line.

headline: Wahkpa Chu'gn: The 2,000-Year-Old Buffalo Jump Behind Havre's Mall
summary:
## Prehistoric Hunters and the Cliff That Fed a People

Behind the Holiday Village Shopping Center on Highway 2 at the western edge of Havre lies one of the most significant prehistoric archaeological sites in the northern Great Plains. The Wahkpa Chu'gn buffalo jump is over 2,000 years old and is recognized as one of the largest and best-preserved bison kill sites anywhere on the continent.

For thousands of years, Plains peoples used the technique of driving bison herds toward cliffs or steep embankments, where the animals would tumble over the edge and be killed or immobilized by the fall. At Wahkpa Chu'gn—the name is from the Assiniboine language—hunters would guide entire herds across the prairie and over the precipice along the southern edge of the Havre Badlands, a formation that runs beside the Milk River west of the city. The bison were then processed below: skinned, butchered, and preserved in a massive communal undertaking.

Archaeological excavation has revealed evidence of the Besant, Avonlea, and Old Women's/Saddle Butte cultural complexes—successive prehistoric peoples who used this site across two millennia. The depth of the bone beds indicates repeated, organized use over generations, making it a primary document of Indigenous life on the northern Plains before European contact.

The Havre Area Chamber of Commerce now oversees the site, which operates as an outdoor museum and tourist attraction. Guided tours take visitors through the excavation trenches during summer months. The juxtaposition of the ancient site against a modern shopping mall parking lot is jarring but telling: this stretch of the Milk River has been a focal point of human activity—for hunting, trading, and settlement—for a very long time.

headline: Havre Beneath the Streets: The Underground City That Fire Built
summary:
## When the Business District Burned, Havre Went Below Ground

In 1904, fire swept through Havre's above-ground commercial district, destroying block after block of the wooden false-fronted buildings that lined First Street. The disaster left the young railroad town without most of its legitimate businesses—but it turned out the town already had a shadow economy waiting below.

For years, an informal underground network had operated beneath Havre's sidewalks, accessed through trap doors and basement entrances. The subterranean world included a brothel, a Chinese laundry, a saloon, a drugstore, and at least three opium dens. These establishments served railroad workers, travelers, and the rougher elements of frontier life, operating largely outside the scrutiny of the law. Small squares of purple glass set into some sidewalks downtown were skylights that let in a trickle of light to the underground rooms below.

After the 1904 fire, legitimate merchants moved their operations underground while new brick buildings were constructed on the streets above. For a period, Havre's entire commercial life—legal and illicit—occupied the same subterranean passages. The arrangement lasted long enough to become part of the city's identity. During Prohibition, the underground rooms found new purpose as spaces for smuggling and consuming alcohol.

The underground area, now designated Havre Beneath the Streets, operates today as a guided tourist attraction at 120 Third Avenue, at the corner of Highway 2 and Third Avenue. Visitors tour the preserved rooms—including a reconstructed brothel, opium den, and druggist's shop—accompanied by guides who interpret the layered history of a city that literally had two faces: the respectable brick storefronts above, and the raw frontier economy thriving beneath.

headline: James J. Hill and the Great Northern Railway's Havre Hub
summary:
## The Empire Builder's Midpoint and the City It Created

James J. Hill—known as the Empire Builder—was a Canadian-born railway entrepreneur who built the Great Northern Railway across the northern United States without a single dollar of federal land-grant subsidy. When his engineers found Marias Pass in northwestern Montana in December 1889, they secured the lowest-grade crossing of the Rockies available to any transcontinental line, and the route they traced through north-central Montana put Havre at the exact geographic midpoint between Seattle and Minneapolis-Saint Paul.

Hill placed his locomotive shops and a major repair facility at Havre—a strategic decision that transformed what had been a few ranch claims into a genuine railroad city almost immediately. The Great Northern's arrival in the early 1890s brought hundreds of workers, a commercial district, hotels, saloons, and all the infrastructure of a functioning town. Havre was incorporated on September 5, 1893, largely because the railroad had given it the population to justify the step.

For much of the twentieth century, the Burlington Northern (successor to the Great Northern) was the largest employer in Havre, sustaining a middle-class workforce of mechanics, engineers, dispatchers, and yard workers. The railroad's physical presence shaped the city's layout: Main Street ran parallel to the tracks, the commercial district fanned out from the depot on Third Avenue, and the social rhythms of the town were tied to the shift schedules of the rail yard.

Hill died in 1916, but his memory is kept permanently in Havre. A bronze statue of Hill stands near the Havre Amtrak station, and Great Northern steam locomotive No. 2584—a 4-8-4 Northern type—sits on permanent outdoor display beside it. Amtrak's *Empire Builder*, named for Hill, still stops at Havre daily on its Chicago-to-Seattle run.

headline: Rocky Boy's Reservation: How Fort Assinniboine Land Became a Homeland
summary:
## Chief Stone Child, Landless Cree, and Montana's Smallest Reservation

For decades after Fort Assinniboine was established in 1879, bands of landless Chippewa and Cree people camped within the fort's enormous military reservation—refugees from eastern territories where their traditional lands had been taken, and from Canada, where the suppression of the Northwest Resistance of 1885 had scattered Métis and Cree communities across the border. The Army tolerated their presence without offering a formal solution.

Chippewa chief Asiniiwin—known to settlers as Rocky Boy, or Stone Child—spent years petitioning the federal government for land and education for his people. He found allies among prominent white Montanans, including former Fort Benton trader and state legislator Frank B. Linderman, who lobbied Congress on his behalf. Rocky Boy died in 1916, just months before Congress authorized the reservation he had sought his entire adult life.

In 1916, Congress set aside a portion of the decommissioned Fort Assinniboine military reservation for what became Rocky Boy's Indian Reservation—the only reservation in Montana named for a specific chief. The land, in Hill and Chouteau counties southwest of Havre, was allocated for the landless Chippewa band. Landless Cree and Métis refugees from Canada also settled there, creating the mixed Chippewa Cree community that inhabits the reservation today.

At 171 square miles, Rocky Boy's is the smallest reservation in Montana by land area, yet it holds a dense history: it is the product of decades of displacement, persistent advocacy, and the repurposing of military land that was originally seized from Plains Indians in the first place. The Chippewa Cree Tribe headquarters is located in Box Elder, just south of the reservation's border with Hill County.

headline: Montana State University–Northern: Havre's Academic Anchor Since 1929
summary:
## A Land-Grant Mission on the Hi-Line

Montana State University–Northern, known locally as MSU-Northern, was founded in Havre in 1929, initially as Montana State Normal School. The institution was established to train teachers for the rural schools of northern Montana—a region where one-room schoolhouses still outnumbered consolidated districts and where qualified instructors were chronically scarce. The Hi-Line's sparse population and dependence on agriculture made teacher preparation a practical necessity, not an abstract educational ideal.

Over the following decades, the school evolved its mission as the region's economy changed. It absorbed programs in vocational-technical education, engineering technology, and—notably—diesel mechanics and automotive technology, fields that reflected the needs of the ranching and railroad communities that surrounded it. MSU-Northern became one of the largest providers of diesel technician training in the Rocky Mountain West, sending graduates to work on the BNSF Railway lines that run through Havre, on the truck fleets that serve Hi-Line agriculture, and on the mining and energy operations of the broader region.

The institution also served as a military contractor, hosting programs for the Army and Air Force at various points during and after World War II. Its campus on the north side of Havre became a fixture of the city's identity, providing employment, cultural programming, and an influx of students from across the Hi-Line and beyond.

Today MSU-Northern is a comprehensive four-year university offering degrees in business, education, health sciences, technology, and the liberal arts. It remains one of Havre's largest employers and a central institution in a city of fewer than ten thousand people—a university that has never stopped being shaped by the specific needs of the northern Montana landscape around it.

headline: The 1904 Fire That Transformed Havre's Downtown Forever
summary:
## How a Conflagration Gave Birth to a Brick City—and an Underground One

In 1904, roughly a decade after Havre was incorporated, fire swept through the commercial district along First Street, consuming the closely packed wooden buildings that had characterized the frontier boomtown. The blaze was not unusual for the era—nearly every western town of the 1880s and 1890s had been built fast and cheap, with tar-papered wood-frame structures that offered little resistance to fire. What made Havre's experience distinctive was what happened next.

As flames destroyed the above-ground businesses, merchants and saloon keepers retreated into the basement-level spaces that had already been operating beneath the sidewalks—the informal underground economy that had flourished since the town's earliest days. For a period after the fire, Havre's entire commercial life operated underground while a new, fire-resistant brick district was constructed above.

The rebuilt downtown that emerged from the 1904 fire was a different city.
